dishesHub.title: Dish guides
dishesHub.intro: Taiwan has one of the world's great street food cultures — and understanding it dish by dish is the fastest way to eat better. These guides cover the history, the best places to try each dish, how to order in Chinese, and what separates the excellent from the average.
dishesHub.whatMakesDiffP1: Taiwanese cuisine sits at a crossroads. It draws from Hokkien and Hakka cooking traditions brought by Han settlers, absorbs fifty years of Japanese colonial influence (you can taste it in the clean broths, the pickled sides, the obsession with quality ingredients), and layers on waves of influence from Mainland China — the beef noodle soup that's now Taiwan's national dish arrived with soldiers from Sichuan in the late 1940s.
dishesHub.whatMakesDiffP2: The result is a cuisine that's simultaneously humble and technically demanding. A bowl of lu rou fan (braised pork rice) costs NT$50 at a street stall and has been perfected by the same vendor for three decades. Xiao long bao — soup dumplings — require a practised hand and years of training to make properly. Both exist in the same city, often on the same street.
dishesHub.whatMakesDiffP3: The flavour building blocks you'll encounter again and again: soy sauce (醬油), rice wine (米酒), sesame oil (麻油), five-spice powder (五香粉), and a heavy hand with fresh scallion. Learn to recognise those and you'll start to understand why Taiwanese food tastes the way it does.
dishesHub.firstTimeBody: Start with beef noodle soup — it's the dish most visitors eat first and the one most likely to permanently ruin restaurant noodles back home. Follow with lu rou fan and a scallion pancake breakfast. Three dishes that cover the full range of what Taiwanese cooking does best.
dishesHub.streetFoodBody: Oyster omelette and scallion pancakes are quintessential night market eating — messy, cheap, and impossible to replicate outside Taiwan. The oyster omelette guide covers exactly which texture to expect and how to tell a good one from a soggy disappointment.
dishesHub.longerStaysBody: If you're spending more than a week, the bubble tea guide teaches you to customise your order the way locals do — sweetness level, ice level, which tea base to choose, and which chains are actually worth visiting versus the ones tourists queue for by mistake.
dishesHub.howWeWriteP1: Every guide on Taiwan Trip Advice is researched in person. We eat at the places we recommend — multiple visits, different days, different times of day. We include Traditional Chinese characters throughout so you can point at menus or show your phone to vendors. We note price ranges in New Taiwan Dollars (NT$) and flag when hours or locations are subject to change.
dishesHub.howWeWriteP2: We don't accept payment for recommendations. The rankings and picks are based on flavour, consistency, and value — nothing else. When a restaurant declines in quality, we update the guide.

nightMarketsHub.title: Night market guides
nightMarketsHub.intro: Night markets (夜市, yèshì) are the beating heart of Taiwanese food culture — outdoor bazaars that open around sunset and run until midnight or later, selling everything from stinky tofu to fresh-squeezed sugarcane juice. Here's how to navigate every major one.
nightMarketsHub.whatToKnowTitle: What to know before you go
nightMarketsHub.cultureBody: Night markets aren't just tourist attractions — they're where Taiwanese families eat dinner, teenagers hang out, and office workers grab supper. The food is cheap (most items NT$50–150), portions are small by design so you can try multiple things, and the atmosphere is genuinely chaotic in the best way. Go hungry. Go with cash.
nightMarketsHub.tip1: Cash only — most vendors don't accept cards
nightMarketsHub.tip2: Go at 8–10pm — peak atmosphere; 6pm is too quiet
nightMarketsHub.tip3: Order one item per stall — don't load up; keep moving
nightMarketsHub.tip4: Avoid weekends if you hate crowds
nightMarketsHub.tip5: Follow the queues — a line means the food is worth it
nightMarketsHub.tip6: Wear comfortable shoes — you'll walk 2–3km easily
nightMarketsHub.taipeiMarketsP1: Shilin and Raohe are the two giants. Shilin is Taiwan's most famous and most visited — massive, touristy in places, but genuinely excellent food if you know which stalls to target. Raohe is calmer, more local-feeling, and easier to navigate in an evening. If you have time for only one Taipei market, Raohe gives most people a better experience.
nightMarketsHub.taipeiMarketsP2: Taipei markets tend to have stronger Japanese and modernised influences — you'll find more fusion snacks, trendy desserts, and Instagram-friendly presentation. Prices are slightly higher than in the south.
nightMarketsHub.southernMarketsP1: Tainan's Huayuan Market and Kaohsiung's Liuhe Market reflect a different food culture — older recipes, more emphasis on seafood, stronger Hokkien flavour traditions. Tainan is considered the most historically significant food city in Taiwan; even the snacks carry centuries of history.
nightMarketsHub.southernMarketsP2: Liuhe in Kaohsiung is famous for its seafood — whole grilled fish, fresh oysters, sea cucumber soup — at prices that feel impossibly cheap by any global standard. If seafood is your priority, Liuhe over Shilin, every time.
nightMarketsHub.foodsTitle: Foods worth seeking at any market
nightMarketsHub.stinkyTofuDesc: Fermented, deep-fried, and served with pickled cabbage and chilli. The smell is confronting; the taste is addictive. Don't let the odour stop you.
nightMarketsHub.scallionPancakeDesc: Flaky, layered, often topped with egg. One of Taiwan's great breakfast and midnight snack crossovers. Order it with egg (加蛋, jiā dàn).
nightMarketsHub.oysterVermicelliDesc: Thin wheat noodles in a thick, slightly gelatinous broth with fresh oysters and pork intestine. A Taiwanese comfort food staple that costs around NT$60.
nightMarketsHub.bubbleMilkTeaName: Bubble milk tea 珍珠奶茶
nightMarketsHub.bubbleMilkTeaDesc: Night market tea shops let you customise sweetness and ice to a degree most tourists don't know to ask for. Always order "少糖少冰" (less sugar, less ice) at minimum.
nightMarketsHub.grilledCornDesc: Brushed with soy sauce, sesame oil, and butter, then chargrilled. It sounds simple. It tastes better than it has any right to. A universal night market presence.
nightMarketsHub.pineappleCakeDesc: Buttery shortbread filled with pineapple jam (sometimes mixed with winter melon). The definitive Taiwanese souvenir pastry — buy them fresh at a bakery stall, not pre-packaged.
nightMarketsHub.shilinTitle: Shilin Night Market: the complete guide
nightMarketsHub.raoheTitle: Raohe Night Market: smaller, better, less crowded
nightMarketsHub.comparisonTitle: Shilin vs Raohe: which market is actually better?
nightMarketsHub.huayuanTitle: Huayuan Night Market: Tainan's best kept secret
nightMarketsHub.liuheTitle: Liuhe Night Market: seafood heaven in Kaohsiung

citiesHub.title: City guides
citiesHub.intro: Taiwan is a small island with enormous regional food variation. What you eat in Tainan tastes nothing like what you eat in Taipei — different ingredients, different traditions, different obsessions. Each guide covers the city's defining dishes, the best neighbourhoods to eat in, and the restaurants worth making a reservation for.
citiesHub.regionalDiffP1: Taiwan's food culture divides roughly north–south, with the biggest distinction between Taipei and Tainan. Taipei absorbed Japanese influence heavily during the colonial period and later developed a cosmopolitan restaurant scene with strong Chinese regional diversity. Tainan preserved older Hokkien and Taiwanese Aboriginal food traditions; its dishes are often sweeter (more rock sugar), more labour-intensive, and deeply local.
citiesHub.regionalDiffP2: Kaohsiung's coastal identity means seafood dominates in a way it doesn't elsewhere. Taichung sits in the middle — geographically and culinarily — with a thriving modern food scene built on excellent local ingredients from the surrounding agricultural plains.
citiesHub.taipeiDesc: Taiwan's capital is a city of contrasts: Michelin-starred restaurants two blocks from a NT$60 bowl of braised pork rice. The beef noodle soup scene alone justifies a trip. Start in Da'an District for cafés and Japanese-style izakayas, then head to Zhongshan for traditional breakfast shops that open at 5am.
citiesHub.tainanDesc: Taiwan's oldest city is where the food obsession goes deepest. Tainan has more traditional Hokkien dishes per square kilometre than anywhere else in Taiwan — and locals are fiercely proud of them. Breakfast culture here starts before 7am with 虱目魚粥 (milkfish congee) and the city is famous for sweets that appear nowhere else.
citiesHub.taichungDesc: Taichung is where bubble tea was invented — the city takes that claim seriously, and the tea shop competition here is fierce. Beyond drinks, Taichung has developed Taiwan's most vibrant independent café culture, a strong Japanese-influenced bakery scene, and night markets that feel less touristy than Taipei's.
citiesHub.kaohsiungDesc: As a port city, Kaohsiung's food identity is built on the sea. The Liuhe Night Market is one of Taiwan's best for fresh seafood at reasonable prices — whole grilled fish, steamed clams, sea cucumber soup. Beyond seafood, Kaohsiung's Hakka communities have preserved cooking traditions that are harder to find in the north.
citiesHub.whichCityTitle: Which city should you visit first?
citiesHub.whichCityFirstTime: If this is your first time in Taiwan:
citiesHub.whichCityFirstTimeBody: Start with Taipei. The infrastructure for visitors is excellent, the food variety is unmatched, and it's the easiest entry point. Two or three days of eating seriously in Taipei will give you a strong foundation for understanding what Taiwanese cuisine actually is.
citiesHub.whichCityDoneTaipeiBody: Go south. Tainan in particular is a revelation for anyone who assumes they've "done" Taiwanese food — it's a completely different register. Take the high-speed rail (HSR) from Taipei; Tainan is under two hours away.
citiesHub.whichCitySeafoodBody: Kaohsiung. The combination of Liuhe Night Market and the city's seafood restaurants makes it the best destination on the island for fresh fish, shellfish, and everything that comes from the surrounding waters.

bubbleTea.title: Ordering bubble tea like a local
bubbleTea.intro: Bubble tea was invented in Taiwan in the 1980s and it's been perfected here ever since. Here's how to navigate the menu without defaulting to whatever sounds familiar.
bubbleTea.sweetnessIceIntro: Every Taiwanese bubble tea shop lets you customize sweetness and ice level. Most tourists order full-sweet, full-ice and wonder why it tastes like sugar syrup. Here's what locals actually order:
bubbleTea.sweet100Label: Full sweet — tourist default. Too much.
bubbleTea.sweet25Label: Light sweet — lets the tea shine.
bubbleTea.iceNoneLabel: No ice — room temp, full flavor.
bubbleTea.localDefaultText: 少糖 (50% sweet), 微冰 (30% ice). This is what regulars order without thinking about it.
bubbleTea.classicMilkTeaDesc: Black tea with creamer (not real milk at most shops) and tapioca pearls. The original. Still the best.
bubbleTea.tieguanyinDesc: Made with roasted oolong instead of black tea. Deeper, more complex, slightly nutty. The connoisseur's choice.
bubbleTea.freshMilkTeaDesc: Real fresh milk instead of creamer. Costs more, tastes better. Worth the upgrade at any serious shop.
bubbleTea.lemonGreenTeaDesc: No milk. Refreshing, tart, and criminally underrated. The best hot-weather order.
bubbleTea.brownSugarDesc: Tiger-stripe caramel, fresh milk, tapioca pearls. Instagram-famous for a reason — but skip the tourist traps.
bubbleTea.passionFruitDesc: A Taiwanese staple you won't find abroad. Bright, tropical, and naturally sweet. No milk needed.
bubbleTea.chunShuiTangNote: Claims to have invented bubble tea in 1986. Classic recipe, slightly sweet. Good starting point.
bubbleTea.tigerSugarNote: The brown sugar tiger stripe original. Long queues, worth it once.
bubbleTea.yifangNote: Fresh fruit teas using Taiwanese fruit. Lighter and more seasonal than milk tea chains.
bubbleTea.fiftyLanNote: The local chain everyone drinks. No frills, consistent, NT$50–80. Order the Assam milk tea.

beefNoodle.title: Best Beef Noodle Soup in Taipei — Ranked & Mapped
beefNoodle.intro: We ate at 12 beef noodle soup spots across Taipei so you don't have to. Here's who won — with neighborhoods, prices, and exactly what to order.
beefNoodle.verdict1: The best in the city. Clear broth, hand-cut noodles, beef that falls apart with a chopstick. Unassuming fluorescent-lit space, open until 5am. This is the benchmark against which all others should be measured.
beefNoodle.order1: Half-tendon, half-beef (半筋半肉). Add a side of braised cucumber (滷小黃瓜) and a cold century egg.
beefNoodle.crowd1: Locals and in-the-know food journalists. Occasional celebrity chef sighting.
beefNoodle.note1: No sign in English. Look for the long queue on Anhe Road at night.
beefNoodle.verdict2: Rich, complex red-braised broth with a slight sweetness from rock sugar and a depth that takes hours of simmering to achieve. The most photogenic bowl on this list. Has been here since 1963.
beefNoodle.order2: Spicy beef noodle (辣牛肉麵). Their chili oil is house-made and excellent. Get it medium.
beefNoodle.crowd2: Everyone — tourists, expats, and regulars who've been coming for 20 years.
beefNoodle.note2: On Jinshan South Road. Expect a 20–30 min queue at lunch.
beefNoodle.verdict3: Humbler presentation than the top two, but the clear broth depth is extraordinary. Under-the-radar and proud of it. The tendon here has the best texture on this list — soft through the center, with a firm exterior edge.
beefNoodle.order3: Clear broth (清燉) — it's where they shine. Don't bother with the red braise here.
beefNoodle.crowd3: Neighborhood regulars. You'll likely be the only non-local in the room.
beefNoodle.note3: Closes by 2pm. Get there before 11:30am for the best broth.
beefNoodle.verdict4: The spiciest bowl on this list by a wide margin. Sichuan-influenced with a numbing heat that builds over the course of the meal. Not authentic Taiwanese style, but outstanding in its own right.
beefNoodle.order4: Spicy red-braised with extra chili (特辣紅燒). Cold sesame noodles (涼麵) as a side.
beefNoodle.crowd4: Spice-seekers and younger locals. Lively atmosphere.
beefNoodle.note4: Near Zhongzheng Memorial Hall MRT. Good for late night — open until 1am.
beefNoodle.verdict5: Best value on this list. The broth is a half-and-half blend of clear and red-braised styles — unusual, and it works. Noodles are machine-cut but better quality than most. A reliable weekday lunch.
beefNoodle.order5: The house mixed-style (招牌牛肉麵). Ask for wide noodles (寬麵).
beefNoodle.crowd5: Office workers and regulars. Fast service, high turnover.
beefNoodle.note5: Cash only. Seating is tight — be prepared to share a table.
beefNoodle.verdict6: The most upscale setting on this list — clean, modern, no plastic stools. The broth is refined and slightly lighter than the traditional red-braised style. A good choice if you want to impress someone who might normally skip noodle shops.
beefNoodle.order6: Premium beef shank noodle (頂級牛腱麵). The shank is sliced thin and fanned — aesthetics matter here.
beefNoodle.crowd6: Xinyi office workers, couples on semi-casual dates.
beefNoodle.note6: Accepts credit cards. Reserve a table for dinner on weekends.
beefNoodle.faq1q: What's the difference between red-braised and clear broth beef noodle soup?
beefNoodle.faq1a: Red-braised (紅燒) uses soy sauce, bean paste, spices, and often a touch of sugar, creating a dark, rich, complex broth. Clear broth (清燉) is a pure beef bone and slow-cooked broth with minimal seasoning — it showcases the meat's natural flavour and is considerably harder to do well. Both are great; the choice depends on what you're in the mood for.
beefNoodle.faq2q: How much should I expect to pay for a bowl?
beefNoodle.faq2a: NT$150–NT$280 for a standard bowl at a sit-down shop. The baseline is usually NT$180–220. Paying more than NT$300 at a non-hotel restaurant is unusual unless it's a premium spot or includes extras.
beefNoodle.faq3q: What's the best part of the beef to order?
beefNoodle.faq3a: Half-tendon, half-beef (半筋半肉) is the default recommendation — you get the collagen-rich chew of the tendon alongside the tender braised beef. Pure beef shank (牛腱) is great if you want something more uniform. Avoid pure tendon only unless you specifically love the texture.
beefNoodle.faq4q: Is beef noodle soup a breakfast food?
beefNoodle.faq4a: A few shops open early and serve it for breakfast, but it's primarily a lunch and dinner dish. Most of the best spots open at 10:30–11am and close once the broth runs out — which at the best places can be as early as 2pm.
beefNoodle.faq5q: Can I get vegetarian beef noodle soup?
beefNoodle.faq5a: A few dedicated vegetarian shops serve mock-beef (素牛肉麵) using gluten or soy protein. The broth is mushroom and vegetable-based and surprisingly complex. Look for shops with 素食 signs.
